narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical level for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile, including your early and later school grades, LSAT score, family income bracket, and planned full-time enrollment, and it predicted a 90.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting those likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The three biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 school decile ranking, and your LSAT score, in that order of influence. Your Year 3 decile of 1—far below the average of about 5.5—had the strongest negative impact, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in school placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers who typically pass the bar. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 1, also well below the average around 5.7, reinforced this concern by showing consistently low early rankings relative to other students. Your LSAT score of 27, much lower than the dataset average of 36.7, further pushed the prediction toward failure, as higher scores are strongly linked to bar passage in the model's view of historical patterns. While your middle family income and full-time status (common among 93% of students) didn't sway things much, these academic red flags dominated the decision. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have mid-to-high deciles and stronger LSATs, your profile stood out as riskier despite your respectable undergrad grades. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—many in similar situations have improved—but the model saw clear hurdles based on these key metrics. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward or above 36-37, while addressing any factors behind those low school deciles through targeted academic support or additional coursework. Reapplying after demonstrating improvement could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at this or other schools. You're capable of turning this around with strategic steps.
